topic: The Mystery of How Lunar Eclipses Work
title: Why the Moon Turns Dark, Copper, and Red During a Lunar Eclipse

lesson.learn: A lunar eclipse happens when Earth moves directly between the Sun and the Moon, casting a shadow across the lunar surface. The Moon usually does not disappear completely because some sunlight is bent and filtered through Earth's atmosphere before reaching it. That filtered light is what gives a total lunar eclipse its red or copper color. Lunar eclipses are predictable because they depend on orbital geometry rather than rare coincidence.
lesson.simpler: A lunar eclipse happens when Earth blocks sunlight from reaching the Moon. The Moon can look red because Earth's atmosphere bends some sunlight into the shadow.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Lunar eclipses are shadow events caused by the Sun-Earth-Moon alignment, and Earth's atmosphere gives the eclipsed Moon its color.

thirdImageCredit.creator: Terry Ballard from Merrick, New York, USA

lesson.deeperDive: The Moon crosses Earth's shadow only when its orbit lines up closely with the plane of Earth's orbit around the Sun. Because the Moon's orbit is tilted, eclipses do not happen every full moon. During totality, Earth's atmosphere filters out blue light more strongly than red light, so the Moon is illuminated by a deep reddish glow rather than going completely black. The exact shade depends on dust, clouds, and volcanic aerosols in Earth's atmosphere at the time of the eclipse. That makes each eclipse slightly different, even though the geometry is the same.
lesson.funFact: If you could stand on the Moon during a lunar eclipse, you would see a solar eclipse caused by Earth.
references: Astronomical almanacs and eclipse geometry references

places: Earth's umbra - the darkest shadow region

Earth's atmosphere - the source of the red glow
